mainEntity.name: Texas refinery exposure years ago, now I have heart problems—is there a statute of limitations?

acceptedAnswer.text: The clock usually starts when you receive a medical diagnosis linking your condition to the past job. However, absolute 'statutes of repose' may apply, so you must consult a lawyer immediately to see if you can still file.
acceptedAnswer.description:
Occupational lung diseases often develop over decades. In Idaho and Colorado, the "statute of limitations" generally starts from the date of diagnosis, not the date of exposure. However, there is often a "statute of repose" which can put an absolute cap on claims (e.g., 10-15 years after the last day of work). If you are just now experiencing heart or lung issues from a refinery job years ago, you must act fast.

Considerations for old claims:
- Scientific evidence linking your specific heart/lung issue to the chemicals used in the past.
- Whether the employer is still in business or has a successor insurance company.
- The availability of the industrial records from that era.

Check with a doctor specifically about "occupational heart disease," which can be linked to certain heavy metal or solvent exposures. Because these cases involve "latency," the legal window is very narrow. Contacting a lawyer immediately is necessary to determine if the statutes have already expired.
